content:
# Genesis Chapter 43 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 25v–26r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_043_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_043_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE — LXX | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0047 (page 43), https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0048 (page 44)

---

## The Famine Will Not Wait

**1** The famine was severe in the land. **2** And when they had finished eating the grain they had brought from Egypt, their father said to them, "Go back, buy us a little more food."

---

## Judah Speaks

**3** But Judah said to him, "The man solemnly warned us, saying, 'You will not see my face unless your brother is with you.' **4** If you are willing to send our brother with us, we will go down and buy you food. **5** But if you will not send him, we will not go down — for the man said to us, 'You will not see my face unless your brother is with you.'"

**6** And Israel said, "Why did you bring this trouble on me by telling the man you had another brother?"

**7** They answered, "The man questioned us carefully about ourselves and our family — 'Is your father still alive? Do you have another brother?' And we told him the truth according to these questions. How could we possibly know that he would say, 'Bring your brother down'?"

---

## Judah's Surety

**8** Then Judah said to his father Israel, "Send the boy with me. Let us get up and go, so that we may live and not die — both we and you and also our little ones. **9** I myself will be surety for him. You may hold me responsible for him. If I do not bring him back to you and set him before you, then I will have sinned against you all my days. **10** For if we had not delayed, we could have gone there and back twice by now."

---

## Jacob Relents

**11** Then their father Israel said to them, "If it must be so, then do this: take some of the finest products of the land in your bags, and carry them down as a gift to the man — a little balm and a little honey, tragacanth resin and laudanum, pistachios and almonds. **12** Take double the silver with you, and return the silver that was put back in the mouth of your sacks — perhaps it was an oversight. **13** Take your brother too, and get up, go back to the man. **14** And may El Shaddai grant you mercy before the man, so that he will release your other brother and Benjamin. As for me — if I am to be bereaved, I will be bereaved."

---

## The Brothers Arrive

**15** So the men took this gift, and they took double the silver with them, and Benjamin. They got up and went down to Egypt and stood before Joseph. **16** When Joseph saw Benjamin with them, he said to the steward of his house, "Bring the men inside. Slaughter an animal and prepare it, for the men will dine with me at noon." **17** The man did as Joseph said and brought the men to Joseph's house.

---

## Fear at the Door

**18** Now the men were afraid when they were brought to Joseph's house. They thought, "It is because of the silver that was returned in our sacks the first time — he is going to set upon us, overwhelm us, seize us as slaves, and take our donkeys." **19** So they approached the steward of Joseph's house and spoke to him at the entrance of the house. **20** They said, "Please, my lord — we came down the first time to buy food, **21** and when we came to the lodging place we opened our sacks and there was each man's silver in the mouth of his sack — our silver in full weight. We have brought it back with us, **22** and we have brought additional silver with us to buy food. We do not know who put our silver in our sacks."

**23** He said, "Peace to you. Do not be afraid. Your God and the God of your father has put treasure in your sacks for you — I received your silver." And he brought Simeon out to them.

---

## Welcome and Washing

**24** The man brought the men into Joseph's house. He gave them water and they washed their feet, and he gave fodder for their donkeys. **25** They prepared their gift for Joseph's arrival at noon, for they had heard they were to eat there.

---

## Joseph Enters

**26** When Joseph came home, they brought him the gift that was in their hands and bowed down before him to the ground. **27** He asked them how they were and said, "How is your aged father you told me about? Is he still alive?" **28** They said, "Your servant our father is well — he is still alive." And they bowed and prostrated themselves.

---

## The Sight of Benjamin

**29** Then he looked up and saw his brother Benjamin — his own mother's son — and said, "Is this your youngest brother, the one you told me about?" And he said, "God be gracious to you, my son."

**30** Then Joseph hurried out, for his compassion was stirred toward his brother and he was about to weep. He went into a private room and wept there.

---

## The Meal

**31** Then he washed his face and came out. He composed himself and said, "Serve the food." **32** They served him separately, and them separately, and the Egyptians who ate with him separately — for the Egyptians could not eat bread with the Hebrews, because that is an abomination to the Egyptians. **33** And they sat before him, the firstborn according to his birthright and the youngest according to his youth. And the men looked at one another in astonishment.

**34** He sent portions to them from his own place, and Benjamin's portion was five times greater than any of theirs. And they drank and were merry with him.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### "The famine was severe in the land" — hunger as the engine of salvation

**וְהָרָעָב כָּבֵד בָּאָרֶץ** — "And the famine was heavy in the land."

The word **כָּבֵד** (*kaved*) — heavy, severe, burdensome — is the same word used for Pharaoh's hardened (*kaved*) heart in Exodus, for the heaviness of Egypt's plagues, for the weight of glory in later theological usage. Here it describes the weight of hunger pressing down on the land of Canaan. The famine is not merely inconvenient; it is existentially threatening. The word does not allow Jacob to keep his youngest son safely at home indefinitely.

This opening verse has one function: to establish that the delay is now impossible. In Genesis 42:38 Jacob closed the door absolutely — "My son will not go down with you." In Genesis 43:1-2 the narrator does not argue with Jacob; the famine argues for him. You cannot hold your beloved child back when the alternative is starvation. The **כָּבֵד** — the heaviness — will eventually prove heavier than the father's fear.

The structural pattern of Genesis as a whole involves God working *through* the pressures of history rather than around them. The famine is real and severe. God did not manufacture it solely to force Jacob's hand; it is part of the historical texture of ancient Near Eastern life. But the narrator has embedded it in a story in which it becomes the pressure that drives Israel toward Egypt, toward reunion, toward the beginning of all that will happen in Exodus. The weight of the world, placed against the weight of a father's love, will eventually tip.

---

### Judah's surety — the transformation begins here

**אָנֹכִי אֶעֶרְבֶנּוּ** — "I myself will be surety for him."

The verb **עָרַב** (*arav*) — to be surety, to pledge oneself, to guarantee with one's person — is the language of personal collateral. A surety (*arev*) stands in the legal place of another person: if that person fails to fulfill an obligation, the surety fulfills it instead. Judah is not merely expressing optimism; he is making a legally binding pledge of himself for Benjamin.

This is the moment the Joseph narrative has been slowly building toward through three chapters: the difference between Reuben's offer and Judah's. Reuben said in 42:37: *put my two sons to death if I do not bring him back.* Judah's offer is structurally different at the deepest level: Reuben pledged his sons — collateral that costs him something but is not himself. Judah pledges *himself* — **אָנֹכִי** (*anokhi*, I myself, the emphatic personal pronoun) — his own person, his own freedom, his own standing.

The word **אָנֹכִי** opens the pledge and is the first word of Judah's sentence. This is not accident. **אָנֹכִי** — the longest and most emphatic form of "I" in Hebrew — is the same word that opens the Ten Commandments: **אָנֹכִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ** ("I am the LORD your God"). The pronoun carries a weight of selfhood, of total personal identification with what follows. Judah is putting the full weight of his own self behind this pledge. *I. Myself. Will be surety.*

The contrast with chapter 37 is devastating. In Genesis 37:26-27, Judah proposed selling Joseph: *What profit is it if we kill our brother? Come, let us sell him.* He reduced his brother to a commercial transaction. Now he offers himself as the price for his brother's safety. The distance Judah has traveled between those two speeches is the moral arc of the entire Joseph narrative's subplot. He will travel it to completion in chapter 44, where he offers himself as a slave in Benjamin's place — the full embodiment of what he here only pledges.

**וְחָטָאתִי לְךָ כָּל-הַיָּמִים** — "then I will have sinned against you all my days." Judah uses the language of sin (*chet'*) — not merely failure or disappointing outcome but transgression against his father. He accepts that if Benjamin does not return, his guilt will not be a completed event but a permanent condition: *all my days*. This is the vocabulary of a man who understands what it means to carry guilt through years — a man who has been carrying the guilt of Genesis 37 for over two decades.

---

### Jacob's gift list — the geography of abundance in a time of scarcity

**מֵזִמְרַת הָאָרֶץ בִּכְלֵיכֶם** — "some of the finest products of the land in your bags."

The word **זִמְרַת** (*zimrat*) in this phrase is one of the most debated words in the chapter. It appears in Hebrew in only one other place — Exodus 15:2 and its parallel Psalm 118:14 — where it means "strength" or "song" in the phrase **עָזִּי וְזִמְרַת יָהּ** ("my strength and the song/strength of the LORD"). In Genesis 43:11, the context requires it to mean "choice products, best things, noble produce." Most translators render it "finest" or "choice products" or "best" — the cream of what Canaan still has to offer despite the famine.

Jacob's list of gifts is remarkable:
- **צֳרִי** (*tsori*) — balm, resin, probably the famous balm of Gilead, a medicinal and aromatic product
- **דְּבַשׁ** (*devash*) — honey, or perhaps a thick date syrup
- **נְכֹאת** (*nekot*) — tragacanth, a gum resin from thorny plants
- **לֹט** (*lot*) — laudanum, ladanum, a resin from Cistus plants
- **בָּטְנִים** (*botnim*) — pistachios
- **שְׁקֵדִים** (*shkedim*) — almonds

These are exactly the goods the Ishmaelite caravan was carrying when it passed through Dothan in Genesis 37:25 — **נְכֹאת וּצְרִי וָלֹט** — "tragacanth and balm and laudanum" — the cargo that the caravan bore south to Egypt when the brothers had just thrown Joseph in the pit and were deciding what to do with him. Jacob is unknowingly sending to Egypt the same category of goods that accompanied his son into slavery. The irony is a slow, silent knife: the father is packing the same aromatics that perfumed the caravan that stole his son.

The inclusion of **דְּבַשׁ** (honey) is notable: in the land flowing with milk and honey, even the honey must be packed and sent south as tribute to the man who holds the grain. Canaan has been reduced to its exotic luxury goods; Egypt has the grain. The power dynamic of the entire narrative is embodied in Jacob's gift list.

---

### El Shaddai — the patriarch's last invocation

**וְאֵל שַׁדַּי יִתֵּן לָכֶם רַחֲמִים לִפְנֵי הָאִישׁ** — "And may El Shaddai grant you mercy before the man."

This is one of only two El Shaddai invocations in the Joseph story (the other being Jacob's blessing in 48:3). The name **El Shaddai** carries the deepest covenantal resonance: it is the name God used in his self-revelation to Abraham (17:1) and Isaac (28:3 — the very blessing Isaac gave Jacob before Jacob went to Laban) and which God himself identifies as the name by which he was known to the patriarchs before the revelation of YHWH (Exodus 6:3).

When Jacob says "El Shaddai," he is not using a generic divine epithet. He is invoking the specific God of covenant promise, the God who promised that the family would become a great nation, the God whose purpose is wrapped up in the survival of these sons. The invocation acknowledges the limit of Jacob's power: he can send the gift, he can send the double silver, he can send his son — but the outcome is beyond his reach. **El Shaddai** is the name for the one who operates at the level where Jacob cannot.

The word Jacob uses for what he is asking El Shaddai to grant is **רַחֲמִים** (*rachamim*) — mercy, compassion, the plural of **רֶחֶם** (*rechem*, womb). The plural **rachamim** is the most intense form of the word: it means something like "womb-love, the love of a mother for a child she carried" — the deepest possible affective connection expressed as compassion. Jacob is asking El Shaddai to make the Egyptian official feel *rachamim* for the brothers. He is asking, not knowing that the man before whom his sons will stand is the son for whom Jacob himself has been weeping for two decades, and who weeps privately every time he sees them.

**וַאֲנִי כַּאֲשֶׁר שָׁכֹלְתִּי שָׁכָלְתִּי** — "As for me — if I am to be bereaved, I will be bereaved."

This line is Jacob's surrender. The verb **שָׁכֹל** (*shakol*, to be bereaved, to be robbed of children) appears twice in immediate succession — the infinitive absolute followed by the perfect — the grammatical construction of absolute resignation. *If bereaved I will be bereaved.* It is not hope; it is the acceptance of the worst. Jacob has finally decided that the possibility of total loss is preferable to the certainty of starvation. He releases Benjamin into the journey not with confidence but with the flat grammar of someone who has run out of alternatives.

The phrase echoes Esther 4:16 — **וְכַאֲשֶׁר אָבַדְתִּי אָבָדְתִּי** ("if I perish, I perish") — Esther's surrender to the possibility of death before approaching the king unbidden. Both Jacob and Esther speak in this idiom at the moment they stop holding back and let go of control. Both are about to discover that the loss they feared will not materialize — but neither can see that yet.

---

### The steward's extraordinary speech

**שָׁלוֹם לָכֶם אַל-תִּירָאוּ אֱלֹהֵיכֶם וֵאלֹהֵי אֲבִיכֶם נָתַן לָכֶם מַטְמוֹן בְּאַמְתְּחֹתֵיכֶם** — "Peace to you. Do not be afraid. Your God and the God of your father has put treasure in your sacks for you."

The steward's speech is one of the most theologically remarkable sentences in Genesis. An Egyptian official — a member of Pharaoh's household, a man with no claim to the covenant of Abraham — is explaining to the sons of Israel that the silver in their sacks is not a clerical error but divine provision. He names their God: **אֱלֹהֵיכֶם** (*Eloheikhem*, your God) and **אֱלֹהֵי אֲבִיכֶם** (*Elohe avikhem*, the God of your father). He speaks of the silver as **מַטְמוֹן** (*matmon*, hidden treasure, a buried cache) — not overpayment, not a mistake, but a gift of concealed wealth.

How does the steward know this? The reader knows: he works for Joseph, and Joseph arranged the return of the money. Joseph told him to put the silver back. So the steward's theology is accurate, just differently grounded than he realizes: the silver was returned at Joseph's order, and Joseph is acting as the instrument of God's provision. The steward perceives the theological reality correctly — this is divine gift — while misattributing the mechanism to divine mystery rather than his master's deliberate care.

The phrase **אֶת-כַּסְפְּכֶם בָּא אֵלָי** — "your silver came to me" — is the steward's quiet reassurance: the commercial transaction was complete and legitimate. He received the silver. They owe him nothing. What is in their sacks now is something beyond payment.

The scene is a small cameo of how God works in the Joseph narrative: through people who are doing their jobs, making their decisions, following their master's instructions — and the theological meaning is present throughout without requiring supernatural intervention. The steward, an Egyptian, becomes the bearer of a message about Israel's God that the brothers needed to hear: *your God has been providing for you even in what you thought was threatening*.

---

### Vayyechmeruv rachamav — Joseph's interior breaks

**וַיִּכָּמְרוּ רַחֲמָיו אֶל-אָחִיו** — "His compassion was stirred toward his brother."

The verb **כָּמַר** (*kamar*, Niphal) — translated variously as "stirred, burned, yearned, was moved" — appears in only two other places in the Hebrew Bible: once in 1 Kings 3:26, where the true mother of the disputed baby cries out because her **rachamim** (compassion) "burned" for her son, and once in Hosea 11:8, where God himself says his heart "recoils" with compassion for Israel. Each time the verb is used, it describes an involuntary, overwhelming upwelling of the deepest love — the kind of love that cannot be held back, that rises from the body's center.

The noun paired with it — **רַחֲמָיו** (*rachamav*, his compassion, his womb-love) — is the same root Jacob invoked in his prayer to El Shaddai just verses earlier: *grant them rachamim before the man.* Jacob prayed that the Egyptian official would feel rachamim for his sons. The narrator now shows us the rachamim that was already there, building in Joseph's chest from the moment he saw Benjamin in the doorway.

The words come in this order: **וַיִּכָּמְרוּ רַחֲמָיו** — "his womb-love stirred, burned" — and then **אֶל-אָחִיו** — "toward his brother." The delay of "his brother" to the end of the clause performs the narrative: the emotion precedes the identification of its object. First the rising of the involuntary feeling, then the naming of what caused it: his brother. Benjamin. The full brother. The one born of Rachel. The one who is the only living connection to his mother.

Joseph has been managing himself through this entire encounter. He checked his emotion when he first saw his brothers in chapter 42 — went into a side room and wept, then came back and continued the test. Now he sees Benjamin and the management fails. He doesn't weep in front of them. He hurries out — **וַיְמַהֵר** (*vaymahèr*, he hurried, he rushed) — into an inner room, a private chamber, and weeps there. The weeping of chapter 43:30 is the most private of Joseph's multiple weepings: alone, away from all eyes, in the one moment where the mask of Egyptian official completely falls and he is only a brother who has not seen his little brother in twenty-two years.

---

### "God be gracious to you, my son"

**אֱלֹהִים יָחָנְךָ בְּנִי** — "God be gracious to you, my son."

This short sentence — six Hebrew words — is one of the most emotionally loaded phrases Joseph speaks across the entire Joseph narrative. He is speaking to Benjamin, his full brother, the only other son of Rachel, a young man who was a child when Joseph last saw him and is now presumably in his early twenties. He addresses him as **בְּנִי** (*beni*, my son) — but Benjamin is not Joseph's son, he is his brother. The word **בְּנִי** here carries the weight of care, protection, and tenderness across a vast age and experience gap. Joseph, who in Egypt has essentially become a new person — Egyptian official, husband, father — is in this single address reverting to family. He is the older brother using the tender word a father would use.

The blessing itself — **אֱלֹהִים יָחָנְךָ** (*Elohim yachanekha*, may God be gracious to you) — is the Jussive form of **חָנַן** (*chanan*, to be gracious, to grant favor), the root of the word **חֵן** (*chen*, grace, favor) that runs throughout the Joseph narrative. Joseph is invoking blessing on Benjamin, asking God to be gracious to the young brother who stands before him. And then Joseph must leave before his face breaks — because the blessing, which is the most he can say without revelation, has nearly spent him.

There is an exquisite irony in the blessing's form: it is a prayer. Joseph is asking *God* to be gracious to Benjamin. But the grace Benjamin will most need in the days to come — the grace that will determine whether he returns safely to his father, whether the cup-in-sack accusation is resolved, whether the family is reunited — will come through Joseph. Joseph is praying for a grace he is in a position to give. He just does not yet know how to give it, or when, or how to survive the giving.

---

### To'evah — the abomination of the shared table

**כִּי-תוֹעֵבָה הִוא לְמִצְרַיִם** — "for that is an abomination to the Egyptians."

The word **תּוֹעֵבָה** (*to'evah*) — abomination, something ritually or morally repugnant — is one of the most charged words in the Hebrew Bible. In Leviticus and Deuteronomy it describes practices that violate the covenant order: idolatry, sexual transgressions, ritual impurity. In Proverbs it describes things the LORD himself finds detestable. The word carries a tone of visceral revulsion.

Here it is used to describe the Egyptian cultural practice of refusing to eat at the same table with Hebrews. The Egyptians cannot eat bread with the Hebrews — it is **to'evah** to them, a categorical taboo. The scene is arranged by the narrator in a precise geometry of separation: Joseph eats by himself, the brothers eat together, and the Egyptians present eat separately. Three tables, three groups, organized by an ethnic and religious hierarchy that the Egyptian host has constructed.

The scene carries a complex irony. The word **to'evah** in the Hebrew Bible is normally used by Israelites to describe what *they* find abominable — it is Israel's own theological and cultural category for the repugnant. Here it is applied to an Egyptian cultural attitude toward *Israelites*. The word used by Israel's tradition to describe idolatrous or immoral practices is turned by the Egyptian tradition against Israel itself. To eat with a Hebrew is, in the Egyptian frame, as transgressive as eating an unclean food.

But the deeper irony is this: the man sitting alone at his own table, separated from both his brothers and his Egyptian staff, is himself a Hebrew. Joseph eats alone — not because he is Egyptian enough to find the Hebrews abominable, but because he is too Hebrew to eat with the Egyptians without breaking, and too Egyptian in his current identity to be seated with his brothers without revealing himself. The three-table arrangement is Joseph's predicament made spatial: he belongs completely to neither world and is not yet ready to cross from one to the other.

---

### The seating in birth order — the impossible coincidence

**וַיֵּשְׁבוּ לְפָנָיו הַבְּכֹר כִּבְכֹרָתוֹ וְהַצָּעִיר כִּצְעִרָתוֹ** — "And they sat before him, the firstborn according to his birthright and the youngest according to his youth."

Joseph has arranged his brothers in birth order. He is the only person in Egypt who knows their birth order — he grew up with them. The brothers, looking at their seats, find themselves placed in the exact sequence of their birth, in the home of a man they believe they have never met before.

The narrator reports their response with a single word: **וַיִּתְמְהוּ** (*vayyitmahu*, they were astonished, amazed, stupefied). The word from the root **תָּמַהּ** (*tamah*, to be astonished, bewildered) — it means a shock strong enough to temporarily freeze thought. They cannot explain what they are seeing. Is it luck? Is it Egyptian perception of age? Does this official have supernatural knowledge? Are they in the presence of someone who knows more about them than they know themselves?

The answer, of course, is yes. But they cannot reach it. The knowledge is right in front of them and they cannot read it. This scene — the brothers astonished at their seating, unable to explain how an Egyptian would know their birth order — is the Joseph narrative at its most artfully constructed. The revelation is available. The clue is in the chairs. But the brothers do not take the last step, and the narrative will not force it for them until chapter 45.

The reader, of course, sees it all. The reader watches ten men sit down to eat, look at each other across their in-order seats, and be astonished — and the reader knows. The dramatic irony of the Joseph narrative reaches its most acute pitch in this single verse. The men are seated in the exact order that proves who arranged the seating, and they do not know.

---

### Benjamin's fivefold portion — the reversal of the reversal

**וַתֵּרֶב מַשְׂאַת בִּנְיָמִן מִמַּשְׂאֹת כֻּלָּם חָמֵשׁ יָדוֹת** — "And Benjamin's portion was five times greater than any of theirs."

The word **מַשְׂאַת** (*mas'at*) — portion, lifting, share — comes from **נָשָׂא** (*nasa*, to lift, carry, bear), the verb of carrying gifts. The portions were "lifted" from Joseph's table and carried to each man's place. Benjamin's portion lifted was five times what any other brother received.

The number five in the ancient Near East is associated with the hand (five fingers) and thus with the power of gift-giving. But in this context it is primarily a narrative signal: Joseph is not yet able to speak to Benjamin, embrace him, reveal himself — but he can do *this*. He can send five times as much to his little brother. He can load Benjamin's plate until it is impossible to miss. The fivefold portion is Joseph's love, displaced and encoded, sent down the table in portions of food because it cannot yet travel as speech.

There is also a testing dimension: Joseph is watching to see how the brothers respond to Benjamin's conspicuous favoritism. In chapter 37, their hatred of Joseph had roots in exactly this — Jacob's favoritism, the special coat, the particular love Joseph was given. Now Joseph is recreating the conditions of favoritism, dramatically, with the son who *replaced* Joseph in Jacob's affections. How will the brothers respond when one of their number is given five times as much? The brothers, the narrator reports, drank and were merry. No resentment. No simmering glance. No muttering at the disparity. The brothers who once could not speak peaceably to Joseph because of his father's love (37:4) now sit and feast and celebrate as Benjamin's plate towers over theirs.

Something has changed. The question is whether it has changed enough — and chapter 44 will test it to the limit.

---

### "And they drank and were merry with him"

**וַיִּשְׁתּוּ וַיִּשְׁכְּרוּ עִמּוֹ** — "And they drank and were drunk with him."

The verb **שָׁכַר** (*shakar*) — to be drunk, to drink freely, to be merry with wine — is the word that closes the chapter. Most translations soften it to "merry" or "freely," but the Hebrew root is the same as *shikkor* (a drunkard) and **שֵׁכָר** (*shekar*, strong drink). They were genuinely and freely drinking, and what they were drinking made them glad.

After years of famine, after the terror of being accused as spies, after the detention of Simeon, after the dread of the returned silver, after Jacob's grief and Judah's pledge and the long road south — here at the table of the man they fear, surrounded by the luxury of Egypt, their brother Simeon finally restored to them, Benjamin seated safely beside them, an official whose face radiates something they cannot quite name smiling at their youngest brother — they drink and they are glad.

The chapter that began with the **כָּבֵד** (heaviness) of the famine ends with the **שָׁכַר** (merryness) of the feast. The weight has briefly lifted. The tears have been privately wept. The food and the wine are real, and the men around the table are their brothers, and for this one evening — before chapter 44 begins and the test grows severe — there is nothing to do but drink.

---

### Structure of the chapter — the grammar of approach

Genesis 43 moves in a structure of concentric approach. The chapter begins at maximum distance — Canaan, famine, the locked house, the father who will not let his son go — and ends at the closest possible proximity: brothers and the man they do not know, sharing food and drink, in a room together, Benjamin's plate heaped five times over.

**Movement 1 — The Deadlock Broken (vv.1–14):** The famine forces the issue; Judah breaks the impasse with his surety pledge; Jacob releases Benjamin with the grief-surrender of El Shaddai invocation.

**Movement 2 — Descent and Arrival (vv.15–17):** The brothers descend to Egypt, present themselves; Joseph sees Benjamin and immediately moves to hospitality.

**Movement 3 — Fear at the Threshold (vv.18–25):** The brothers misread Joseph's invitation as trap; the steward's extraordinary reassurance converts their terror into relief; Simeon is restored.

**Movement 4 — The Meal (vv.26–34):** Progressive intimacy: the bow, the question about the father, the sight of Benjamin, the private tears, the composed return, the meal, the birth-order seating, the fivefold portion, the drinking.

The entire chapter is a movement from locked distance to unlocked proximity. Jacob, who said at the end of chapter 42 that Benjamin would not go down, sends him. The brothers, who were turned away from Egypt's grain by their guilt and fear, are now inside Joseph's house eating at his table. Simeon, who was bound before their eyes, is now sitting among them unbound. Benjamin, who could not leave Canaan, is the guest of honor at an Egyptian official's feast.

The chapter does not resolve — the resolution requires chapter 45. But it moves. It moves because Judah spoke, and because the famine was heavy, and because El Shaddai was invoked, and because Joseph's compassion was stirred, and because ten men who were once guilty of throwing their brother in a pit sat down and ate bread with his ghost and were glad.

---

### Manuscript observations

The Leningrad Codex folio 25v (GEN_043_page_001.jpg) is the recto side of the leaf photographed in the second page image, continuing the text from the end of Genesis 42 (visible in the final lines of the right column) directly into Genesis 43. The transition is marked with a standard Masoretic **פ** (petuhah, open section) marker. The LC page shows three columns of densely set Hebrew text in the Tiberian tradition, with full vocalization throughout.

The opening words of Genesis 43 — **וְהָרָעָב כָּבֵד** (*vehara'av kaved*, "and the famine was heavy") — are visible at the top of the right column on folio 25v in the West Semitic Research Project photograph. The *dagesh forte* in the *bet* of **כָּבֵד** (*kaved*, heavy) that distinguishes this form is visible in the careful Tiberian pointing.

Judah's surety speech in verses 8-10 occupies a prominent section of the right column. The key phrase **אָנֹכִי אֶעֶרְבֶנּוּ** (*anokhi e'ervennu*, "I myself will be surety") is legible with the characteristic *aleph-nun-kaf-yod* spelling of the emphatic first person pronoun and the Qal imperfect of *arav* with third masculine singular suffix.

The El Shaddai invocation in verse 14 — **וְאֵל שַׁדַּי** — is visible in the text, the divine name standing in characteristic form with *aleph-lamed shin-daleth-yod* consonants and the distinctive Masoretic pointing.

The central emotional scene — **וַיִּכָּמְרוּ רַחֲמָיו** (v.30, "his compassion was stirred") — falls in the left column of the first manuscript page at approximately mid-column. The rare Niphal form of **כָּמַר** with its distinctive pointing is legible in the vocalized text.

The folio 26r (GEN_043_page_002.jpg) carries the second half of Genesis 43 from approximately verse 19 onward, continuing across both columns and into Genesis 44. The three-table meal description in verse 32 — with the phrase **כִּי-תוֹעֵבָה הִוא לְמִצְרַיִם** (*ki to'evah hi leMitsrayim*, "for that is an abomination to the Egyptians") — is visible in the right column of folio 26r with the distinctive **ת-ע-ב** root of *to'evah* legible in the unpointed consonantal text and confirmed by the Tiberian vocalization above.

The astonishment verse — **וַיִּתְמְהוּ** (*vayyitmahu*, v.33) — appears as a single rare verb form in the column. The root **ת-מ-ה** (*tamah*, to be astonished) appears fewer than two dozen times in the Hebrew Bible, making this occurrence a notable hapax in the Joseph context.

The closing verse — **וַיִּשְׁתּוּ וַיִּשְׁכְּרוּ עִמּוֹ** (*vayyishtu vayyishkeru immo*, v.34) — is legible at the end of the chapter section in folio 26r, the chapter ending with the two *vav*-consecutive verbs of drinking and inebriation before the text flows into the beginning of Genesis 44 in the following lines.

Notable LXX (Vaticanus) variants for Genesis 43:

**V. 9** — The Vaticanus rendering of **אָנֹכִי אֶעֶרְבֶנּוּ** (*I myself will be surety for him*) uses **ἐγὼ δέ ἀναδέχομαι αὐτόν** — "but I myself take him on, undertake for him." The verb **ἀναδέχομαι** (*anadechomai*) — "to take up, undertake, accept responsibility" — is the LXX translator's Greek equivalent for the Hebrew commercial-legal term of suretyship. The LXX preserves both the emphatic personal pronoun **ἐγώ** (*ego*, I) and the sense of personal assumption of responsibility. The Vaticanus uncial page (pa_0043, continuing from the Genesis 42/43 boundary) renders this speech with no textual deviation from MT.

**V. 14** — Vaticanus renders **El Shaddai** as **ὁ θεός μου** (*ho theos mou*, "my God") rather than a transliteration. The LXX tradition across Genesis renders *El Shaddai* variably: sometimes as **θεὸς παντοκράτωρ** (*theos pantokrator*, "God Almighty"), sometimes as simply "God." In Genesis 43:14, Vaticanus uses the shorter form, which loses the specific covenantal resonance of the Shaddai epithet that the Hebrew preserves.

The word **ἐλεήσει** (*eleēsei*, "will have mercy on you") in the same verse is Vaticanus's rendering of the Hebrew prayer that God will grant **רַחֲמִים** (*rachamim*). The Greek word **ἐλέος** (*eleos*, mercy) is the LXX's standard translation of **חֶסֶד** (*hesed*, covenant-faithfulness) and **רַחֲמִים** (*rachamim*, compassion/womb-love). The nuance of **rachamim** — the visceral, maternal, embodied quality of the compassion Jacob invokes — is somewhat flattened in the broader Greek mercy-word, though **ἐλεήσει** carries its own theological depth as the root of the NT word **ἐλεέω** (*eleeo*), used in the Kyrie Eleison and the healing cries of the blind men before Jesus.

**V. 23** — The steward's speech in Vaticanus reads **ἵλεώς ἐστε, μὴ φοβεῖσθε** — "Be gracious, do not fear" — where the first word **ἵλεώς** (*hileōs*) translates **שָׁלוֹם** (*shalom*). The LXX translator has rendered *shalom* not as **εἰρήνη** (*eirēnē*, peace) but as **ἵλεώς** (*hileōs*, propitious, gracious, favorable) — a word used in contexts of divine favor and priestly blessing. The steward's greeting to the frightened brothers is rendered in theological rather than merely relational terms: the situation is *favorable*, God is *propitious* toward you.

The identification of the God of Israel — **ὁ θεὸς ὑμῶν καὶ ὁ θεὸς τοῦ πατρὸς ὑμῶν** (*ho theos humōn kai ho theos tou patros humōn*, "your God and the God of your father") — follows the MT closely, with the Greek article distinguishing the personal God of the covenant family from any other deity.

**V. 30** — For the rare verb **וַיִּכָּמְרוּ** (*vayyikameru*, "his compassion was stirred"), Vaticanus reads **ἐταράχθη δὲ τὰ ἔγκατα αὐτοῦ** — "and his intestines/inward parts were disturbed/troubled." The word **ἔγκατα** (*enkata*, inward parts, guts, entrails) renders **רַחֲמָיו** (*rachamav*, his womb-love/compassion). The LXX translator has located the emotion anatomically — not in the womb but in the intestines/guts — and rendered the emotional upheaval as physical disturbance (**ἐταράχθη**, *troubled, agitated*). This is a fascinating translation decision: the Greek body-vocabulary for intense emotion uses **ἔγκατα** (guts) where Hebrew uses **רֶחֶם** (womb/compassion). The anatomy is different but the visceral quality is preserved: something in the deepest interior of Joseph's body moved involuntarily when he saw Benjamin.

**V. 33** — The astonishment at the birth-order seating: Vaticanus renders **וַיִּתְמְהוּ** as **ἐξέστησαν δὲ οἱ ἄνθρωποι** — "and the men were amazed, were beside themselves." The verb **ἐξέστησαν** (*exestēsan*) — from **ἐξίστημι** (*existēmi*), to be beside oneself, to be driven out of one's senses — is the same root as the NT word **ἔκστασις** (*ekstasis*, ecstasy, amazement). The brothers' astonishment at the seating is rendered in Vaticanus with the strongest possible Greek word for loss of composure: they were driven outside themselves by what they saw. The LXX preserves the full emotional weight of the scene.

The Vaticanus pages covering Genesis 43 (pa_0043–pa_0044, accessible at https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0047 and https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0048) present the text in continuous uncial script with the characteristic three-column layout of the Genesis sections. Chapter markers in the Vaticanus numbering system appear at the top of pa_0044, consistent with the boundary note in the GEN_042.md manuscript observations that identified the Genesis 42/43 boundary as falling across pa_0043. The Genesis 43/44 boundary falls within pa_0044 in the Vaticanus arrangement.
